data.title: Hong Kong enters summer flu season as Covid infections also climb
data.description:
Hong Kong health authorities have called on the public to stay vigilant as the city has entered the summer flu season, with Covid-19 cases also on the rise.
Dr Albert Au Ka-wing, head of the communicable disease branch of the Centre for Health Protection, said on Thursday that surveillance data suggested flu cases rose significantly last week, signalling the beginning of the seasonal infection period.
He noted that it was difficult to predict how long the season would last, as the city generally entered its winter flu season in the first quarter, but this year had defied the trend, as the previous flu season only ended at the beginning of January.
Au added that the most common strain in the city was the H3 subtype of influenza A, similar to last year.
“So far, around 60 per cent of the infection cases are caused by influenza A subtype H3, similar to last September and December. The vaccines are effective. However, we also observe cases from subtype H1 and influenza B,” he said.
“We will closely monitor the situation. Generally, the summer influenza season lasts for eight to 12 weeks. We expect that influenza activity remains at a relatively active level in August and September.”
Among the respiratory specimens collected by public laboratories last week, 6.69 per cent tested positive for influenza, which was above the baseline of 4.94 per cent.
While 60 per cent of the cases were caused by influenza A subtype H3, the H1 subtype of influenza A and influenza B each took up about 20 per cent of the remaining cases.
Last week, Hong Kong recorded 23 severe flu infections in adults, 70 per cent of whom were patients aged 65 or older. Sixty per cent of the total had not received any jabs under the 2025-26 vaccination programme.
The city also reported two cases of severe paediatric influenza A infection over the past week, including a 12-year-old boy with underlying illness and a six-year-old boy with past good health.
Noting that the pair had received flu jabs last October and November, Au said vaccination offered the best protection in the first six months after injection, but immunity gradually weakened over time.
Au also warned about the infection risks posed by Covid-19, as its activity level had begun to increase from May.
Specimens testing positive for Covid-19 in public laboratories rose from less than 1 per cent before the end of May to 6 per cent last week.

Covid-19 infections in patients admitted to accident and emergency departments at public hospitals reached 11 per 1,000 cases.
He noted that the dominant variant strains were the same as those in the past 18 months, which were descendant lineages of JN.1.
The laboratories have not detected any unusual rise in other strains.
Au urged the public to maintain stringent personal, environmental and hand hygiene at all times to minimise the risk of infection. He also encouraged those with respiratory infection symptoms to wear masks.
Dr Leung Yiu-hong, head of the centre’s emergency response and programme management branch, urged the public to get vaccinated, particularly infants.
According to Leung, about 65 per cent of the child population had been vaccinated, but coverage for infants aged between six months and two years old was only 27 per cent.
He added that the government was preparing for the 2026-27 seasonal flu vaccination programme and would reveal details later.
Leung also said authorities estimated that less than 4 per cent of infants had received their first jabs to protect against Covid-19.
He said the current Covid-19 vaccination programme would end on July 10 for children aged between six months and 11 years, while that for individuals aged 12 and over would conclude on September 5.
He urged the public to get inoculated as soon as possible before the next batch of Covid-19 vaccines arrived in October.
Dr Larry Lee Lap-yip, Hospital Authority’s chief manager for cluster performance, said public hospitals faced pressure from influenza and Covid-19, noting that the in-patient bed occupancy rate had reached 110 per cent.
He added that the authority would plan accordingly and step up its services.

data.title: Rare momentum: Hong Kong universities, schools boost commercial property marketEducational institutions pour more than US$1.4 billion into deals, helping steady the city’s commercial real estate secto
data.description:
Hong Kong’s educational institutions are forecast to ramp up their investment and leasing in the commercial property segment, with leading universities and international schools among those paying top dollar to acquire or lease office and retail spaces, according to Colliers.
In the first five months of the year, property acquisitions by educational institutions accounted for nearly 40 per cent of total investment in the commercial property sector, the consultancy said.
“Education-related investments have already reached HK$11.1 billion [US$1.4 billion] in the first five months of 2026 alone,” said Thomas Chak, head of capital markets and investment services at Colliers.
“This reflects very strong momentum as institutions increasingly shift from leasing to ownership,” Chak said. “The current pace suggests 2026 could be one of the most active years on record for education-driven real estate investment, with transaction volume projected to exceed HK$15 billion.”
The investments had already surpassed the HK$5.6 billion and HK$4 billion such institutions deployed in the property sector in 2024 and 2025, respectively, Colliers said.

Hong Kong’s commercial real estate sector has struggled as offices grapple with a supply glut amid slower demand, while retail properties face pressure from e-commerce and the surge in northbound travel for shopping and dining.
The pickup in education leasing and investment is partially propping up the sector.
Some of the notable purchases include the University of Hong Kong buying an en bloc office property, covering 389,000 sq ft, at 92-103A Connaught Road West in Sheung Wan for HK$3.93 billion in April, and City University of Hong Kong paying HK$1.96 billion for the Festival Walk Office Tower in Kowloon Tong, which has an area of 229,000 sq ft, in January. Neither disclosed the purpose of their purchase.
DSC International School also acquired low-floor spaces measuring 146,000 sq ft at 5-7 Tai Fung Avenue in Taikoo Shing for HK$400 million in May, securing its campus from the landlord.
Harrow International School Hong Kong bought a 52,122 sq ft retail property at 5 Lok Yi Street in Tuen Mun for HK$122 million in March. It did not disclose the purpose.
In leasing, Nord Anglia International School rented 73,800 sq ft of space on the third to sixth floors of Harbourfront Landmark in Hung Hom for a sixth-form centre.
“Transaction evidence shows education occupiers are taking up large floor plates, typically between 25,000 and over 90,000 sq ft per deal,” Chak said.
“Demand is driven by the need for contiguous space for classrooms and facilities, making them comparable to major corporate tenants in scale and requirements.”
Colliers estimated that total leasing volume of education institutions by year-end would be broadly in line with 2025 levels, “given the high base”.
While education institutions were unlikely to replace banking, finance and insurance firms as dominant tenants in prime office spaces in the near term, they were expected to become an “increasingly important demand driver for landlords with high vacancies to reposition their tenant strategies over the next few years”, Chak said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s box office takings for first half of 2026 up 25% year on year
data.description:
Hong Kong’s box office takings for the first half of 2026 rose by nearly 25 per cent year on year, with comedy film Night King emerging as the highest-grossing movie over the six-month period.
According to figures from the Hong Kong Box Office, 142 films premiered between January 1 and June 30, of which 17 were locally made. The movies generated HK$664 million (US$85 million) in takings, up from HK$531 million in the first six months of last year.
“The summer film season has just begun with many family-friendly offerings. The latter half of the year will also boast many high-quality domestic and international movies,” the industry body said on Thursday.
“It is hoped that the summer season and the second half of the year will sustain the strong momentum seen in the first half.”
Night King was the top earner for the first half of 2026, having recorded HK$118 million in receipts so far. The comedy was released on February 17, targeting the Chinese New Year holiday period, and is still screening locally.
The film, starring comedian Dayo Wong Tze-wah and singer-actress Sammi Cheng Sau-man, centres around a once-glamorous nightclub’s fight against a hostile takeover in 2012.
The film is rated category III, meaning it is only suitable for viewers aged 18 and over.
Wong also starred in court drama A Guilty Conscience, which was the city’s highest-grossing film of 2023. It raked in HK$115 million and set a record for the most-watched film in Hong Kong with 1.53 million viewers.
The record was broken the following year by the action film Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In.

The next highest earner for the first half of this year was crime thriller Cold War 1994. The movie has made HK$38 million at the box office since it started screening on May 1.
The movie’s plot loosely follows the real-life story of a tycoon’s kidnapping and features superstars such as Chow Yun-fat, Louis Koo Tin-lok, Aaron Kwok Fu-shing and Fish Liew.
Comedy The Snowball on a Sunny Day, crime thriller We’re Nothing At All and sci-fi flick Ciao UFO took the third to fifth places in the domestic box office, with receipts ranging between HK$11 million and HK$16 million.
The best-performing international film in Hong Kong over the same period was Avatar: Fire and Ash. The sci-fi epic, which screened between January 8 and April 22, achieved box office receipts of HK$40 million.
The second best international earner was family-friendly animation The Super Mario Galaxy Movie, which grossed HK$37 million.
The top two were followed by animations Toy Story 5 and Hoppers, as well as drama The Devil Wears Prada 2, with box office takings that ranged from HK$27 million to HK$33 million.
Hong Kong recorded a 16 per cent year-on-year decrease in box office revenue in the first half of 2025. The percentage stayed the same for the full-year figure.

data.title: Make Hong Kong China’s ‘space finance capital’, legal group urges Beijing
data.description:
An independent legal group has proposed that Beijing designate Hong Kong as the country’s “space finance capital”, positioning the city as a gateway for commercial development of the sector under the national five-year plan.
The Asian Academy of International Law also proposed in policy papers issued on Thursday that Hong Kong should enact a space asset registration and finance ordinance to provide legal certainty for the financing of such assets.
The group pointed to Hong Kong’s key strengths as a world-class international financial centre, a common-law jurisdiction and a connectivity hub, and put forward a series of recommendations to leverage the city as the country’s strategic gateway for commercial space under the 15th five-year plan from 2026 to 2030.
“Mainland China should provide the technology, manufacturing, licensing, and sovereign capability, while Hong Kong provides the capital markets, legal infrastructure, global connectivity, and international credibility to fund, structure, contract, and dispute-resolve China’s commercial space transactions at scale,” it said.
Beijing’s 15th five-year development blueprint has elevated aerospace within the cluster of strategic emerging industries, positioning it as a key driver of new quality productive forces, which refers to productivity led by technological innovation that breaks away from the traditional economic growth and development model.
Public interest in aerospace has grown in Hong Kong following the selection of Lai Ka-ying as the city’s first astronaut. She made her historic journey into space with the launch of the Shenzhou-23 mission in May.
The academy proposed designating Hong Kong as the country’s “space finance capital”, with Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing serving as the preferred offshore listing platform for commercial space firms and the city’s funding structures in the fixed-income and structured-finance capital markets acting as an international capital gateway.


Also among its recommendations was establishing Hong Kong as the default governing law and dispute-resolution jurisdiction for all cross-border commercial space contracts involving Chinese entities.
It also called for the creation of a dedicated Hong Kong-mainland commercial space interface in forthcoming national space legislation, embedding legal recognition of Hong Kong’s complementary role.
Noting the absence of a clear legal framework for recording and prioritising private rights in space assets, the academy proposed that Hong Kong enact a space asset registration and finance law.
“Hong Kong’s experience with aircraft finance shows how a robust legal infrastructure and targeted tax policy can catalyse an industry,” it said.
“The proposed legislation draws on this precedent and adapts features from the Cape Town Convention on International Interests in Mobile Equipment and its Space Protocol, aiming to provide a clear, friendly and efficient legal framework as the foundation of the budding industry of financing space assets.”
The Cape Town convention is an international treaty intended to standardise transactions involving movable property.
According to the Hong Kong-based group, the global space economy is projected to grow from about US$630 billion in 2023 to about US$1.8 trillion by 2035, representing annual growth of roughly 8 to 9 per cent.
China’s commercial space sector is undergoing rapid transformation, with more than 600 commercial space companies, about 18.6 billion yuan (US$2.7 billion) in private financing in 2025, and commercial launches accounting for 54 per cent of the country’s total launch count last year, according to the group.

data.title: Cathay Pacific to resume Middle East passenger flights from September
data.description:
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways will resume Middle East passenger flights in September, six months after suspending services due to the war.
The airline said on Thursday it would progressively restore passenger and cargo operations, starting with freighter flights to Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, from August 1.
Daily passenger flights to Dubai in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and four-times-weekly services to Riyadh will resume on September 1.
“These flights are already open for sale. Cathay will continue to closely monitor the evolving situation in the Middle East ahead of the resumption dates,” the airline said.
A check by the South China Morning Post found that a Cathay round-trip economy flight from Hong Kong to Dubai in September cost up to HK$8,853, with HK$2,524 attributed to carrier surcharges.
Cathay suspended all passenger and cargo flights to and from the Middle East on February 28, following a joint US-Israeli attack on Iran, and has since extended the suspension.
Hong Kong’s Security Bureau has maintained an amber travel alert for Saudi Arabia and the UAE, urging residents to monitor the situation and exercise caution when travelling to these countries.

data.title: New technologies power China’s Tesla challengers to monthly sales records
data.description:
Chinese Tesla challengers Leapmotor and Zeekr, banking on their latest battery and self-driving technologies, bucked a downward trend in domestic electric vehicle sales with record deliveries last month, ratcheting up pressure on the US carmaker amid weak consumer sentiment towards big-ticket items.
Stellantis-backed Leapmotor delivered 93,376 electric vehicles (EVs) last month, up 94.5 per cent year on year, as it rewrote its sales record for a second consecutive month.
Zeekr, a premium EV unit of Geely Auto, China’s second-largest carmaker, also smashed its delivery record for a second straight month in June, reporting sales of 35,169 units, up 110.6 per cent year on year.
“The two carmakers, along with Xpeng, Nio and Xiaomi, are emerging as the winners of China’s EV market this year, but they represent just a small portion of the country’s EV industry,” said Eric Han, a senior ­manager at Shanghai consultancy Suolei. “Their technological advancement and fancy new models effectively spurred a sales jump.”
Tesla has yet to announce last month’s delivery volume in China, but its Shanghai Gigafactory now assembles only Model 3 and Model Y vehicles priced above 200,000 yuan (US$29,441).
Hangzhou-based Leapmotor builds and sells midsize smart EVs at roughly half the price of comparable Tesla models.
In May, Tesla’s Shanghai factory delivered 47,281 vehicles to customers in mainland China, up 22.5 per cent year on year, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association.
Chinese EV makers and component suppliers now dominate the domestic and global EV markets, buoyed by their cost and technological advantages.
Tesla was the front-runner in China’s premium category – where EVs cost more than 200,000 yuan – for five years until Xiaomi’s fully electric SU7 outsold Tesla’s Model 3 last year.
A record 156 new models are expected to hit the mainland’s vehicle market in the second half of the year, with analysts saying that is likely to fuel a new round of price competition as carmakers try to reduce excess inventory.
Global consultancy AlixPartners predicted this week that China’s car sales would fall by 10 per cent this year on the back of a shaky economy and softening government support.
It added that top players could increase their market share because they were better placed to withstand the price war that would ensnare nearly all the country’s 100-odd carmakers.
Shanghai-based Nio delivered 40,597 vehicles to domestic and international customers last month, up 62.9 per cent year on year and its best monthly result of the year.
Guangzhou-headquartered Xpeng, part owned by Volkswagen, also saw deliveries hit a high point for the year, up 15.9 per cent year on year to 40,126 vehicles.
BYD, the world’s largest EV assembler, recorded sales of 403,472 units last month, up 5.5 per cent year on year.
Xiaomi, a smartphone maker and EV start-up, said its June deliveries exceeded 30,000 units.

data.title: Woman accused of money laundering in Wang Fuk Court fire case granted bail
data.description:
A Hong Kong court has granted bail to an assistant manager of the project consultant behind a HK$336 million (US$42.8 million) renovation at the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court, pending a money laundering trial.
Li Min, 33, secured bail at the High Court on Thursday after spending three weeks behind bars in connection with the city’s deadliest blaze in recent history. Her bail money was set at HK$300,000, with her boyfriend also pledging HK$100,000 as surety.
Li was among seven individuals and two companies implicated in the first criminal case against those deemed responsible for the fire that broke out at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po on November 26 last year.
Mr Justice William Tam Yiu-ho ordered Li to surrender her travel documents, reside at her boyfriend’s flat in Sha Tau Kok, report to police three times a week and avoid prosecution witnesses as part of her bail conditions.


Police and the Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) levelled a total of 25 counts against the nine accused, including manslaughter, conspiracy to defraud, money laundering, attempting to pervert the course of public justice and tax evasion.
Li faces a count of money laundering for allegedly using her personal bank accounts to handle more than HK$1.37 million in illegal proceeds on behalf of Will Power Architects from October 16, 2019, to November 30, 2025.
According to the ICAC, Will Power, the consultant behind Wang Fuk Court’s exterior overhaul, made false representations to residents to lure them into picking Prestige Construction and Engineering as the project’s main contractor.
Will Power also allegedly misled building authorities into believing Prestige’s false litigation history was true and that the consultant’s registered inspector had duly discharged his duties in supervising 86 building maintenance projects, including the works at Wang Fuk Court.

In the first hearing at West Kowloon Court on June 10, Chief Magistrate Victor So Wai-tak granted bail to Prestige director Hau Wah-kin, 54, and 41-year-old bank worker Chung So-fan, the wife of Will Power director Wong Hap-yin, 40.
The lower court dismissed the bail applications by Li, Prestige director Gordon Ho Kin-yip, 52, Will Power’s registered inspector Wilson Ng Yeuk, 44, and 60-year-old merchant Hung Kwok-wai, who is Wong’s friend and business partner.
Wong, who faces a total of 16 counts, did not apply for bail. Hung’s second bid for bail was rejected on June 18.
The case will be heard again at West Kowloon Court on September 2.
The 43-hour blaze was the deadliest in Hong Kong since 1948, killing 168 people and leaving nearly 5,000 homeless.
Prestige’s use of substandard scaffolding mesh and polyfoam boards during the works has been cited as contributing to the fire’s rapid spread across seven of the eight 31-storey towers at the estate.
The fire aroused concerns over possible bid-rigging, as the estate’s renovation budget ballooned from a preliminary estimate of HK$152 million in September 2023 to HK$336 million in 2024, when Prestige’s offer - the most expensive option among 57 tenders - was adopted.

data.title: Can China’s memory chip giant CXMT keep thriving after IPO and AI boom?
data.description:
As China’s leading DRAM maker ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) prepares for its expected 29.5 billion yuan (US$4.3 billion) Shanghai Star Market listing, the critical question facing investors is whether the firm can convert its cyclical windfall into permanent industry leadership.
The Hefei-based chipmaker enters the market at a uniquely lucrative moment: a global memory shortage driven by surging artificial intelligence computing demand has triggered a massive upcycle in pricing, delivering windfall earnings.
In the first quarter, CXMT’s revenue jumped 719 per cent year on year, swinging from a loss of 2.83 billion yuan a year earlier to a net profit of 33 billion yuan. Its average selling prices are now within 5 to 10 per cent of global leaders Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix and Micron Technology, according to estimates by research firm SemiAnalysis.
CXMT has started taking orders for its advanced DDR5 server memory products from Tencent Holdings and ByteDance after completing customer validation, according to a person familiar with China’s server memory supply chain, who declined to be named because he was not authorised to speak to the media.
The chipmaker’s customer portfolio spans other tech giants including Alibaba Cloud, Lenovo, Xiaomi, Oppo, Vivo, Transsion and Honor, covering sectors from data centres to personal computers and smartphones. Alibaba Cloud is part of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Amid soaring international component costs, Apple is lobbying Washington for regulatory clearance to buy memory from CXMT, according to recent reports by the Financial Times and Bloomberg.

A blockbuster listing at the top of the cycle
The validation by hyperscalers highlights a shift in the company’s centre of gravity away from low-power mobile memory (LPDDR) for smartphones towards the highly profitable cloud server and AI data centre ecosystem.
Driven by mass production of DDR5 that started at the end of 2024, CXMT’s server-related revenue as a share of its combined memory sales jumped from about 8 per cent in 2024 to over 23 per cent in the first half of 2025.
While CXMT still trailed far behind the top three suppliers, it had taken advantage of this momentum to capture an 8 per cent global DRAM market share in the first quarter, up from 3 per cent a year earlier, according to Counterpoint Research data.
Operating three 12-inch fabs in Hefei and Beijing, with a capacity that ranks first in China and fourth globally, the company said it expected total capacity to exceed 300,000 wafers per month once current facilities fully ramped up.
While anticipation is high for what is set to be one of the largest chip-related initial public offerings (IPOs) in China in recent years, the difficulty in valuing the company highlights the risks for investors.
If CXMT issued 10 to 15 per cent of its post-IPO shares to meet its fundraising target, the implied pricing would create a low-end arithmetic valuation floor of around 197 billion yuan, SemiAnalysis said. However, investors were already whispering about a trillion-yuan valuation.
This large gap highlights the risks for investors who may be estimating future earnings based on peak-cycle numbers: CXMT’s cost per bit for DDR5 memory remained over 30 per cent higher than its global peers, according to SemiAnalysis.
This indicates that its current profit explosion is still driven more by a generous pricing window than by structural cost parity.
Beyond pricing volatility, CXMT is also navigating increasingly hostile geopolitical headwinds. Future expansion, which is expected to include more fab capacity in Shanghai, is threatened by US-led export controls such as the proposed Match Act, which would require allied countries to match US curbs on semiconductor equipment exports to China within 150 days.
Industry analysts pointed out that the primary risk to CXMT was not only whether it could buy new tools, but also whether imported equipment already installed in China would receive the technical support needed for advanced DRAM production.
“The risk is not an instant shutdown, but long-term degradation,” said Leslie Wu, CEO of semiconductor consultancy RHCC, noting that the proposed legislation reflected a shift in US restrictions from equipment sales to the full equipment life cycle.
While machines and production lines may remain in place, advanced manufacturing could become harder to stabilise, replicate and commercialise, Wu said.

HBM: the hardest test
For all its progress in conventional DRAM, high-bandwidth memory remains the clearest test of CXMT’s long-term ambitions.
HBM, which stacks DRAM chips to deliver much higher bandwidth, has become a critical component for AI accelerators and one of the most strategically important products in the memory industry.
For China, the stakes are especially high. US export controls have limited access to advanced AI chips and related memory products, making domestic HBM one of the missing pieces in Beijing’s AI hardware ambitions.
SemiAnalysis said CXMT still faced hurdles in turning HBM into stable commercial supply, noting that it had made progress in its DRAM process technology, but catching global leaders in HBM would require better wafer yields, more mature stacking and more advanced packaging capabilities.
The research firm expects CXMT’s HBM wafer capacity to accelerate in 2027 and 2028, reaching 55,000 wafers per month and 100,000 wafers per month, respectively, raising its share of global HBM wafer supply from about 1 per cent in 2025 to 12 per cent in 2028.
CXMT has not publicly confirmed HBM mass production, customer certification or yield levels.

data.title: Hong Kong assets hit record US$5.38 trillion on renewed China appetite: SFC
data.description:
Assets and wealth under management in Hong Kong surged to a record high last year as global investors returned to Chinese assets, underscoring the city’s edge over Switzerland as a wealth hub.
Financial firms in Hong Kong managed HK$42.2 trillion (US$5.38 trillion) worth of assets last year, a 20 per cent increase from the previous peak of HK$35.5 trillion in 2024, according to a report released by the Securities and Futures Commission on Thursday.
Driven by the asset-management and private-wealth businesses, Hong Kong recorded net fund inflows of more than HK$2 trillion in 2025, soaring 193 per cent, year on year, the city’s securities watchdog said.
The SFC attributed the performance to firm confidence among global investors, market innovation and Hong Kong’s talent pool, which bolstered the city’s resilience against global headwinds.
“Looking ahead, the SFC remains committed to continued regulatory enhancements to foster Hong Kong’s competitiveness as a premier international financial centre and a leading offshore renminbi hub,” Elisa Ng, the SFC’s executive director of investment products, said in a statement.
The commission’s data follows a late-May report from Boston Consulting Group showing that Hong Kong overtook Switzerland to become the world’s top cross-border wealth hub in 2025.
According to the SFC, of the invested assets, HK$4.8 trillion remained in Hong Kong, and HK$2.3 trillion went to mainland China, representing year-on-year increases of about 26 per cent and 30 per cent, respectively. These were the two fastest-growing single regions. In comparison, assets allocated outside the city and the mainland grew by nearly 14 per cent, year on year, to about HK$9 trillion.
The surge was in line with robust gains in Hong Kong and mainland stock markets last year, with the Hang Seng Index and the CSI 300 Index surging 28 per cent and 18 per cent, respectively, after China’s home-grown, low-cost artificial intelligence model, DeepSeek, sparked a broad revaluation of the nation’s tech assets.
Meanwhile, Hong Kong continued to play a critical role in asset diversification amid geopolitical tensions, with 56 per cent of funds invested outside the city and the mainland.
North America and Europe remained the two largest overseas destinations last year, attracting around HK$2.8 trillion and HK$2.2 trillion in assets, respectively. However, despite single-digit increases to those regions of 9 per cent and 2 per cent, the overall proportion of those funds shrank by 2 percentage points and 3 percentage points, respectively, from the year prior.

data.title: Hong Kong retail sales grow 7.9% in May, extending winning streak to 13 months
data.description:
Hong Kong’s retail sales in May rose 7.9 per cent year on year, extending the streak of growth to 13 months, with the sector benefiting from an increase in visitor numbers amid mainland China’s Labour Day “golden week” holiday.
The value of retail sales for the month reached HK$33.8 billion (US$4.3 billion), according to provisional figures released by the Census and Statistics Department on Thursday, compared with HK$31.3 billion in May 2025.
Retail sales for the first five months of the year rose by 10.6 per cent from the same period last year.
“The ongoing economic expansion and sustained growth in local labour earnings, together with continued increase in inbound visitors, should benefit the retail businesses,” a government spokesman said.
“The government will closely monitor the potential implications of the evolving external uncertainties on the local consumption market.”
During the five-day Labour Day holiday between May 1 and 5, Hong Kong welcomed more than 1.01 million mainland visitors, a 10 per cent rise from a year ago, exceeding government expectations.
But at that time, industry leaders said their spending was inconsistent.
Sales of jewellery, watches, clocks and valuable gifts continued to lead growth among major retail categories, surging 25.8 per cent year on year in May.
Sales of “other consumer goods not elsewhere classified” rose 14.8 per cent, while electrical goods and other consumer durable goods increased 13 per cent.
Department stores and optical shops also recorded gains of 9.2 per cent and 10.3 per cent respectively.
Among the weaker segments, sales of Chinese drugs and herbs fell by 9.5 per cent from a year earlier, while fuel sales dropped 12.2 per cent.
Sales of food, alcoholic drinks and tobacco also edged down 0.3 per cent.
Motor vehicles and parts, which had driven retail growth the previous month following buyers rushing to purchase electric cars before the expiry of a tax concession, grew at a more modest 1.7 per cent in May.

data.title: Will slowing car sales in China reignite brutal price war in crowded market?Global consultancy AlixPartners delivers most bearish forecast for Chinese carmakers’ fortunes this year.
data.description:
In the most bearish Chinese car market forecast by an international bank or consultancy, AlixPartners has predicted that deliveries will fall by 10 per cent this year on the back of a shaky economy and softening government support.
Weak buying interest was likely to fuel a brutal price war that would ensnare nearly all the country’s 100-odd carmakers, the global consultancy added.
It forecast that 24.6 million light vehicles would be delivered by Chinese carmakers this year, with 10 million of those to be exported.
“Profitability is no longer driven by scale, but increasingly by how efficiently companies are organised, how quickly they adapt product cycles, and how effectively they integrate design, engineering and commercialisation,” said Stephen Dyer, Asia-Pacific leader of the automotive and industrial practice at AlixPartners. “We expect a widening gap between winners and the rest of the industry, with consolidation becoming a structural outcome rather than a cyclical one.”
Sales of light vehicles – passenger cars and pickups – slumped 18 per cent in the first five months of this year after Beijing adjusted its subsidy policy and phased out a sales tax holiday.
AlixPartners said the predicted 41 per cent year-on-year increase in exports this year, spurred by cost and technological advantages of Chinese-made cars, would not be enough to cushion against an overall fall in sales.
It said the 14.6 million light vehicles expected to be delivered to domestic customers this year would represent a 27.7 per cent year-on-year decline.
Differentiation in new-energy vehicles is becoming harder to sustain as technologies mature and converge
Stephen Dyer, AlixPartners
The consultancy said that of the 30 Chinese carmakers that only make electric vehicles (EVs), just three – BYD, Leapmotor and Xiaomi – were profitable because of fierce price competition that ate into profit margins. Another 20 or so manufacturers make both EVs and petrol-powered cars.
Despite Beijing urging carmakers to quit price wars and avoid heavy losses since the middle of last year, AlixPartners said further price reductions could exacerbate their worsening business outlook.
“Capacity has expanded faster than demand can absorb it, and differentiation in new-energy vehicles is becoming harder to sustain as technologies mature and converge,” Dyer said, adding that budget-conscious consumers would only consider heavily discounted cars in a slumbering market.
He also predicted that only seven of the 30 Chinese carmakers solely focused on EVs would break even by 2030, with small and weak players likely to either exit the market or be acquired by stronger rivals.
Deutsche Bank said in January that passenger vehicle sales in China would fall 5 per cent this year, while UBS ­projected a 2 per cent fall, citing over­capacity and reduced government support.
In April, Morgan Stanley predicted that China’s domestic car sales would remain flat this year.

data.title: Clogged pipeline: Many Hong Kong IPO hopefuls near deadline
data.description:
A large number of mainland Chinese companies hoping to list shares on the Hong Kong stock exchange are anxiously waiting for a nod from the mainland securities regulator, with many now at risk of having their listing applications lapse.
More than 430 companies were currently waiting in the initial public offering (IPO) pipeline, according to data from bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX). More than 30, including mainland supermarket chain Qiandama and EVE Energy, a mainland-listed lithium battery maker with a market capitalisation above 140 billion yuan (US$20.6 billion), faced the six-month expiration of their applications within the next two weeks, the data showed.
The backlog in the first half of the year was the most severe since March 31, 2023, when a mechanism was put in place that requires approval from the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) before HKEX can convene a listing hearing, according to Roy Lo, managing partner of accounting and consulting firm SW Hong Kong.
“The CSRC’s approval criteria are closely tied to national policy,” he said. “As of the first of this year as an example, industries that are “going global” – those that have significant overseas investments and an urgent need for capital support, such as [firms involved in] large-scale artificial intelligence models, robotics, semiconductors and biotechnology – have found it relatively easier and faster to obtain approval.”
If the 30-plus companies nearing the six-month deadline fail to pass their listing hearings in the next two weeks, they will be forced to update their financial data to restart their applications. SW Hong Kong has seen 12 of its own clients submit Hong Kong IPO filings this year, but only two have successfully secured the coveted CSRC clearance.
However, Lo said that expiration does not equate to failure. “Many companies that have successfully gone public have had their prospectuses lapse,” he said. “As long as the company’s fundamentals have not deteriorated, the process can still move forward after updating the information.”
For example, after receiving CSRC approval, Chaozhou Sanhuan Group, a maker of electronic components and advanced materials, quickly updated its financial information and resubmitted its application. It successfully passed the hearing in about two weeks and launched its offering this Tuesday.

Despite the uncertainties caused by regulatory approvals, Hong Kong’s IPO market has continued to perform strongly overall.
A total of 83 companies raised US$26.42 billion on the exchange’s main board from January to June, up about 84 per cent year on year, according to LSEG Data & Analytics.
Faced with unpredictable approval cycles, Lo said that mainland companies currently planning to go public were generally mentally prepared for a “marathon” and expected to need to update their financial information one or two additional times.

data.title: Shanghai unveils quantum computing hub as China races for tech supremacy
data.description:
Shanghai is stepping up its push into quantum computing by launching a dedicated industrial hub in the city, bringing together a group of companies to accelerate the development of the emerging technology and compete with other Chinese cities that have established quantum industry clusters.
The Shanghai Quantum Computing Future Industry Incubation Zone, launched on Tuesday in the city’s Xuhui district, has attracted an initial cohort of 26 quantum firms. The zone would offer resources, talent and subsidies to expedite a shift from laboratory research towards a full-fledged industrial ecosystem for quantum computing, according to a report by the state-backed Jiefang Daily.
The incubation zone would also provide up to 100 million yuan (US$14.73 million) to fund foundational research, technology innovation and platforms. Up to 20 million yuan would be available for companies developing their first products, alongside computing resource subsidies to help “bring down companies’ computing and validation costs”, the Jiefang Daily report said.
Shanghai would intend to use the industrial hub to strengthen its position in quantum computing by integrating research institutions, start-ups and downstream applications, from biomedical research to financial technology, according to the report.
The city would also expect that the “deep integration” of artificial intelligence and quantum technologies would help accelerate technology iteration, real-world testing scenarios and the commercialisation of research results.
The Xuhui district in Shanghai, which already houses the city’s top AI labs including StepFun AI, the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and Hong Kong-listed MiniMax, is seen as a key base for enabling this convergence.

Officials aim to expand Xuhui district’s quantum firms to more than a hundred in the next three years and set a combined valuation target in the hundreds of billions of yuan, the Jiefang Daily said.
The move comes as China has elevated quantum technology to a national strategic priority in its 15th five-year plan – the country’s economic and social development blueprint covering the years between 2026 and 2030 – while local governments across China race to establish quantum industry clusters.
Backed by leading universities and research institutions, Beijing issued a policy in 2023 to support the development of future-facing industries, including quantum computing, with notable start-ups such as QBoson piloting applications of the technology in cancer research and drug development.
Hefei, the capital of China’s eastern Anhui province, is home to over 90 quantum-related companies as of last year, accounting for a third of the country’s total.
Shenzhen, which named quantum computing as one of its eight strategic industries alongside photonic computing, blockchain, spatial technology and neural science, among others, recently secured its first quantum unicorn after SpinQ raised a total of 1 billion yuan (US$147.32 million) in its latest funding round.

data.title: Lawmaker William Wong failed to tell Legco about arrest, set for committee probe
data.description:
Hong Kong lawmaker William Wong Kam-fai failed to report his arrest for alleged drink-driving to the Legislative Council as required under its code of conduct, and is expected to face an internal investigation that could result in stiff penalties, the South China Morning Post has learned.
The 66-year-old lawmaker, who is also an associate dean of engineering at the Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK), was arrested on Monday night after crashing his car into two parked vehicles on campus.
He is suspected of drink-driving, careless driving and failing to stop and report after a traffic accident.
Sources said on Thursday that Wong did not inform the legislature or explain the matter to fellow lawmakers before the incident became public on Wednesday, constituting another breach of the members’ code regarding the “timely reporting of legal proceedings”.
Under the code, lawmakers found guilty of misconduct may face penalties ranging from written warnings to suspension and pay deductions, following an investigation by Legco’s Supervisory Committee.
While the committee typically launches an investigation after receiving complaints, several lawmakers familiar with its operations said they expected it to examine Wong’s case.
The legislators, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said the case was more serious misconduct than that of lawmaker Judy Chan Kapui’s traffic offence earlier this year.
Chan was fined HK$2,000 (US$255) in March after pleading guilty to driving against the flow of traffic, for which the committee also issued her a written warning.
The lawmakers added that Wong had failed to explain the incident transparently, damaging public perception.
Depending on the court’s sentence, they said Wong could face a harsher penalty, and a disqualifying mechanism could be triggered if he received jail time. Drink-driving carries a maximum penalty of a HK$25,000 fine and up to three years’ imprisonment.

Under the Basic Law, lawmakers convicted and sentenced to one month or more in prison for criminal offences may be disqualified if Legco passes a motion to remove them with a two-thirds majority.
The SCMP has sought comment from Legco president Starry Lee Wai-king, who chairs the Supervisory Committee.
Wong apologised on social media on Wednesday for the incident, saying it had caused inconvenience for the legislature, the university and various sectors. He also pledged to fully cooperate with the police investigation.
In response to an SCMP inquiry, he declined to provide further comment, citing the ongoing investigation.
Ronny Tong Ka-wah, a senior counsel and Executive Council member, said drink-driving was a serious offence, and described the incident as a matter of integrity.
The government adviser added that if Wong drove away from the scene in an attempt to lower his blood alcohol concentration or avoid a breathalyser test, it could amount to perverting the course of justice.
In 2024, lawmaker Wong proposed using artificial intelligence to combat drink-driving more effectively.
“Many drivers, tempted by convenience, take the risk of driving home after drinking just one or two glasses of alcohol. Once an accident occurs, it is too late for regrets,” he said at the time.
He had also urged the government to raise public awareness by strengthening education and encouraging the development of designated driver services to help prevent dangerous driving incidents.
Wong is also a member of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC), the country’s top political advisory body. It is currently unclear whether Wong will face immediate expulsion over the incident.
A CPPCC member, speaking anonymously, cited the case of former deputy Witman Hung Wai-man, who held onto his position until the end of his term after breaching Covid-19 regulations in the “partygate” scandal in 2022.
But the member said Wong might not be reappointed when the next term begins in 2028.
CUHK earlier acknowledged the arrest, but declined to comment further, as the case was under police investigation.
Wong has been released on bail and is due to report back to police in late July.

data.title: Beijing’s liaison office in Hong Kong restructured, stressing district work
data.description:
Beijing’s liaison office in Hong Kong has been restructured, with the economic department split into two and its district-level groups expanded, a move that an analyst said reflected the central government’s greater focus on financial, youth, and district work.
A comparison of the office’s organisational chart on its website on Thursday with internet archive records showed that the number of departments had been reduced from 24 to 23, even as its Economic Department was split into two entities, the Economic and Financial Department I and Department II.
The previous Education and Technology Department and Youth Work Department were consolidated into the Education and Youth Department.
The reorganisation also signalled an expansion in district-level coordination, with the New Territories Work Department split into the New Territories East Affairs and West Affairs Department.
The Hong Kong Island and Kowloon Work Department was also renamed slightly as the Hong Kong Island and Kowloon Affairs Department.
The restructuring also introduced a new Social Development Department, running in parallel with the existing Social Works Department.
Three departments, namely the Shenzhen Contact Department, the Information Enquiry Centre and the Information Centre, were removed.

Lau Siu-kai, a consultant to the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said the overhaul signalled a shift in strategy by the central government.
“This shows that the liaison office is placing a much stronger focus on financial, youth, and district work moving forward, which aligns more closely with the Hong Kong government’s policy priorities and underscores how much importance Beijing attaches to these areas,” he said.
“All of these responsibilities involve deep cross-border cooperation, including the internationalisation of the renminbi, supporting the education, employment, and career development of local youth, and strengthening the various ties between Hong Kong residents and the mainland.”

data.title: China’s tax crackdown: A-share and Hong Kong-listed firms hit hard
data.description:
China’s anti-tax evasion campaign is sweeping across the A-share and Hong Kong stock markets, with at least 80 listed firms already having been ordered to pay back corporate income taxes and late fees to local authorities.
The number of listed companies involved in the first half of the year is likely to soon surpass the total of 89 for full-year 2025, raising concerns that Beijing’s taxation drive could be extended amid local debt black holes, which would threaten corporate earnings, cash flows and investor sentiment.
Pharmaceutical, new materials, chemicals, environmental protection, agriculture, and information technology firms accounted for most of the cases, according to Shanghai-based Wind, a Chinese financial market data provider.
“The surge in supplementary tax payments reflects China’s efforts to reduce system leakages amid pressure on the fiscal deficit,” said Gary Ng, senior economist for Asia-Pacific at Natixis.
“The main drag now falls on A-shares, but there could be spillover to Hong Kong-traded shares as well.”
Global oncology developer BeOne Medicines, triple-listed in mainland China, Hong Kong and the US, was among the high-profile names caught up in the compliance wave. Its Chinese subsidiary agreed to pay about 446 million yuan (US$65.6 million) after receiving a notice from a local tax authority, according to a June 26 filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
The payment could potentially affect the company’s operating income this financial year, according to Zhang Jialin, head of China healthcare research at Nomura.
The tax bill is equivalent to about 8 to 9 per cent of BeOne’s estimated full-year operating income in 2026, said Zhang. However, “it is not a reason for investors to worry about the company’s fundamentals”.
State-owned Heilongjiang Agriculture was among the hardest hit. The company was ordered to pay back corporate income tax of 1.02 billion yuan and late-payment surcharges of 386 million yuan for the period from 2021 to 2025, revealed by a filing with the Shanghai Stock Exchange on June 23.
The payment was expected to reduce net profit attributable to Heilongjiang Agriculture’s shareholders by 1.41 billion yuan in financial year 2026. In Shanghai over the past two weeks, the company’s shares hit rock bottom at 10.69 yuan apiece on June 25, before rebounding to 11.03 yuan on July 1.
Other companies on the list included Guangzhou Jinzhong Auto Parts Manufacturing, an industrial components maker; Chengdu Olymvax Biopharmaceuticals, a human vaccines producer; and Lopal Tech, which manufactures automotive fine chemicals and lithium-ion battery cathode materials.
These payments could weigh on investor sentiment in the near term, according to Ng. “Sectors that have previously enjoyed tax benefits may now face greater reversal risks unless the firms are critical to supply chain security,” he said.
“China’s financial market is moving towards stricter compliance rules and tax enforcement, which is not bad if it comes with transparency. But the question is how much leakage needs to be recovered during the transition, which could affect corporate profitability,” Ng added.

data.title: Hong Kong police search 15 in former July 1 protest areas, man taken away
data.description:
Hong Kong police have stopped and searched 15 people in areas where July 1 protests were previously held, with one man wearing a T-shirt with political undertones taken away.
The force said on Thursday that five men and 10 women, aged between 25 and 73, were stopped and searched in Wan Chai and the Eastern district the previous day, when the city marked the 29th anniversary of its return to Chinese rule.
The date also marked the fifth anniversary of a stabbing attack in which a lone assailant wounded a police officer outside a department store before killing himself.
Since then, security has been heightened each year. In recent years, some residents have brought white flowers to commemorate the incident.
On Wednesday evening, police cordoned off the area between East Point Road and Great George Street in Causeway Bay with red tape. Both plain-clothes and uniformed officers were seen patrolling nearby.
Officers also set up a platform equipped with a camera directed at pedestrians.

A 32-year-old man taken from the scene was seen wearing a T-shirt bearing wording with political undertones, apparently mocking the government’s narrative that Hong Kong had entered an era of “prosperity and stability” since its return to China.
Local media reported he had been standing outside Sogo and making a phone call.
Police said the man was taken away for further investigation and later released.
The department store had long been a focal point for July 1 demonstrations, where residents gathered for more than two decades to voice demands and concerns.
The last organised, police-approved July 1 protest took place in 2019.
The Civil Human Rights Front, which had organised the annual marches, disbanded in 2021.

data.title: Temu owner PDD embraces China’s ‘city of the future’ after regulatory debacle
data.description:
The operator of budget online marketplaces Temu and Pinduoduo is ramping up its commitment in a state-backed development zone – dubbed a “city of the future” in official discourse – months after the e-commerce group was hit by the heaviest penalty in a record food-safety crackdown and accused by regulators of obstructing enforcement.
PDD Holdings had more than 600 employees at its unit in the Xiongan New Area by the end of June, making it the development zone’s largest privately owned internet company, according to reporting by the Chinese media outlet Jiemian. The roles include middle-office operations, data analysis and quality-control specialists.
PDD registered a new entity in Xiongan in late May with 500 million yuan (US$73.7 million) in capital and has since moved quickly to publicise its commitment to the area.
The company has launched a hiring drive aimed at creating more than 5,000 jobs locally. In mid-June, PDD said its first 150 employees had completed onboarding, with most coming from the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region. The move aligns with Xiongan’s official role as a key site for absorbing Beijing’s non-capital functions as the state seeks to integrate the regional economy.
PDD’s executive president, Zhu Zheng, met local officials in Xiongan in mid-June and signed the Digital Service Strategic Co-operation Framework Agreement, according to the Xiongan government website. A few days later, PDD bought an office building in Xiongan from the Power Construction Corporation of China, which marked PDD’s first disclosed property purchase there. The company was expecting to move employees into the building by the end of July.
PDD’s highly visible commitment to Xiongan follows a period of severe tension with Chinese regulators.


In April, the State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) imposed 3.6 billion yuan in fines and confiscations on seven platforms operated by PDD, Meituan, JD.com, Alibaba and ByteDance over “ghost-food-delivery” stores. Pinduoduo, PDD’s main domestic platform, received the heaviest penalty, at 1.52 billion yuan. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Friction was further intensified by allegations that PDD had obstructed the investigation. China Quality Daily, a SAMR-supervised newspaper, and Xinhua said that an enforcement official was injured during an altercation at the company. The reports also alleged that an employee swallowed a note that was meant to silence a colleague during regulatory questioning.
For Xiongan, PDD’s arrival comes at a critical moment, as the planned “city of the future” has long faced questions of whether it could attract real businesses, jobs and residents.
President Xi Jinping underlined the importance of industrial development in March during his fourth visit to the area, where he inspected China Huaneng Group, one of the first central state-owned-enterprise (SOE) headquarters to relocate to Xiongan. Since 2023, the number of central SOE entities newly established in Xiongan has nearly tripled to more than 400, according to Xinhua.
Under official plans, Xiongan is expected to be “basically developed into a modern socialist city” by 2035.
PDD’s move echoes an earlier rush by Chinese internet groups into Xiongan after the area’s launch in 2017. Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu and JD Finance were among those that announced local entities or partnerships, but many of the projects have since produced limited exposure of sustained investment, staffing or commercial activity.

data.title: Tighter site safety urged after Hong Kong records over 60 industrial accidents
data.description:
Hong Kong labour unions have urged worksite supervisors to step up risk assessments and precautionary measures to strengthen work safety culture, as the city reported at least 62 industrial accidents, including 24 fatal cases, in the first half of this year.
The calls on Wednesday followed the case of a worker who was killed when an excavator toppled at a Ho Man Tin construction site two days earlier, and of a man who died after being struck by a road roller at a Kowloon Bay refurbishment site on Friday.
Fay Siu Sin-man, chief executive of the Association for the Rights of Industrial Accident Victims, said machinery had long been a major cause of industrial accidents, adding that a lack of risk assessments before work and a failure to correct problematic practices could have led to the casualties.
“The importance of pre-work risk assessments should not be undermined … As long as they identify the risks, they will think about the precautionary measures and minimise the accidents,” she said.
“It is a matter of safety awareness. It is not only about the frontline workers, but all individuals in every work position.”
Siu said a safe work culture could only be built when the site owner provided resources without rushing the job, middle-level management monitored hazards closely, and workers proactively identified risks.
According to data from the association and media reports gathered by the South China Morning Post, Hong Kong recorded at least 62 industrial accidents between January and June from different industries, including 36 cases at construction or maintenance sites.
Among all 62 accidents, 16 involved the use of machinery, including the two that took place over the past week, 15 were falls from a height and 13 resulted from being struck by falling or moving objects.

Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, said accidents involving machinery and falls from a height were common in the sector.
Noting that workers’ negligence might have led to some accidents, Chau also pointed to the absence of a safe work culture and insufficient oversight.
He cited Monday’s excavator accident as an example, in which the operator pressed ahead with a lifting operation on a slope where the soil was likely to have been loosened following heavy rain, resulting in the worker’s death and another being injured.
Chau questioned whether the operator had conducted a risk assessment before work, such as checking slope integrity and loading weight.

He also expressed doubt as to whether the site had taken sufficient safety precautions, such as blocking the lifting area from other workers’ access, and whether it had enough supervisors.
“Some accidents can be avoided when the supervisor is aware of the risk and stops the operation,” he said.
Both representatives also pointed to the potential risk posed by hot weather, as the Hong Kong Observatory warned of record-high temperatures this year and next.
Chau noted that construction site operators had offered drinking water and cooling areas, as well as allowed rest breaks when necessary, but he said workers were often reluctant to take breaks as they feared losing their jobs.
He hoped employers could provide sufficient rest time and more cooling equipment for workers, such as cool towels and shirts with built-in fans, adding that the government could offer subsidies for buying such equipment.
Siu from the association urged site operators to increase the number of water stations and shelter areas, as some workers reported that these amenities were not easily accessible.
She added that the employers could also encourage workers to rest whenever needed and measure their vital signs before work.
Additional reporting by Kate Chan

data.title: Residents, visitors flock to waterfront as PLA navy ships sail into Hong Kong
data.description:
Residents and tourists have flocked to Hong Kong’s harbourfronts to welcome two People’s Liberation Army (PLA) naval vessels on a five-day port call to the city.
The guided-missile destroyer Nanning and the guided-missile frigate Hengyang sailed into Hong Kong via Lei Yue Mun and Victoria Harbour on Thursday morning, with the ships arriving at Ngong Shuen Chau Barracks at 9am and 9.40am respectively.
A PLA band began to play celebratory music to welcome the Hengyang’s arrival at 9am, with dozens of officers waving in formation to greet the frigate. Naval officers in turn waved back from the decks of the frigate as it docked at the barracks.
China’s Ministry of Defence said the port call by the PLA vessels was to mark the 29th anniversary of the city’s return to Chinese sovereignty, which fell on July 1.
The Nanning, commissioned in 2021, was deployed to Sudan in April 2023 to evacuate hundreds of Chinese nationals and foreign citizens during the civil war.

Commissioned in 2008, the Hengyang has taken part in escort missions in the Gulf of Aden and off the coast of Somalia over the years.
Residents and visitors gathered at the East Coast Boardwalk and Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront early on Thursday morning to catch a glimpse of the two vessels entering the city.
Among them was Jason Chan, a 41-year-old clerk, who had waited at the Quarry Bay promenade since 6.30am to see the two ships.
“I didn’t want to miss it. It’s not something you see every day, so I came early to get a good spot,” he said.
While he was unable to secure tickets for the open days, Chan said he hoped to see the “deck equipment, weapons systems and how the sailors operate up close” in the future.

Zhang Yifan, a 29-year-old software engineer from Shenzhen, arrived in Hong Kong on Wednesday for the handover anniversary celebrations and was also at the Quarry Bay promenade.
“I’ve seen naval ships before, but not this close in Hong Kong, so it feels special. When they came into view, you can really feel the scale of them,” he said.
“It felt symbolic, I feel honoured to have witnessed it in person ... it’s a special moment marking Hong Kong’s return to the motherland.”
Armed with photography gear, Ling Yuan, a 30-year-old tourist from Sichuan province who had also travelled to Hong Kong for the handover anniversary celebrations, arrived at the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront at 7am to capture the perfect shot.
“I saw warships in Kennedy Town in 2024, but I’m still excited to see them inside Victoria Harbour, especially with the high-rise buildings in the background,” he said.
Ling added that his experience in Hong Kong was rare, as taking photos of military vessels was prohibited in mainland China.

Zhang Yutong, a 31-year-old mainland student pursuing his PhD in Hong Kong, arrived at the same spot at around 7.30am.
“Apart from the excitement, I feel a deep sense of pride seeing that our country has made progress in the military field and having these warships sail right past this major landmark,” he said.
The military enthusiast said he visited the Ngong Shuen Chau Barracks for three consecutive years and would attend the open day again this weekend.
“As an enthusiast, I roughly know what equipment is on board, but it’s still a rare opportunity. So why not?”
Other passers-by gathered along the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront to capture the navy ships on their phones as the vessels sailed past, with some waving excitedly at the fleet.
Residents can visit the destroyer and the frigate between 8.30am and 5.30pm on July 4 and July 5 at the Ngong Shuen Chau Barracks on Stonecutters Island.

The first batches of 14,000 free tickets to visit the ships – on offer through the PLA Hong Kong Garrison’s WeChat social media account until July 2 on a first-come, first-served basis – were snapped up within minutes of their release on June 30.
To facilitate the arrival and departure of the two vessels on July 2 and 6, some ferry services will be rescheduled or cancelled.
Affected routes include services between Central and Cheung Chau, Mui Wo, Peng Chau, Yung Shue Wan, Sok Kwu Wan, Discovery Bay, and Ma Wan, as well as services between Ma Wan and Tsuen Wan.
